description: An observer disposer latches: the first call removes its registration, every later call is a no-op, and it never removes anything else (#lzdartobservercow). Cell `topic` pins the plain case — `obs_a`'s disposer called three times leaves `obs_b` and `obs_c` untouched and raises no error. Cell `reuse` pins the case that motivated the clause: `obs_m1` is disposed, an equal callable is then registered as `obs_m2`, and `obs_m1`'s disposer is called a *second* time. It MUST do nothing. What breaks otherwise: where removal is keyed by the callback rather than by the registration, an unlatched disposer's second call removes whatever now matches that key — which is a later subscription belonging to a caller that never asked to be unsubscribed. lazily-py found exactly that in `b2de504`. Note the interaction with the no-dedup clause: a latched disposer cannot express this bug no matter how the collection is keyed, which is why bindings SHOULD latch in the disposer rather than relying on the collection. Cell `gone` pins the lifetime edge: disposing the cell drops its observers *without* invoking them, and a disposer called after its cell is gone is a no-op rather than an error — callers hold disposers in teardown paths that run in an order they do not control. Assertions are on which observers fired and on the absence of an error; nothing here inspects the collection or the disposer's representation.

expect.note: repeat disposal is a silent no-op, not an error and not a double-removal
three calls removed exactly one registration
the load-bearing call: obs_m1's disposer is spent, so it MUST NOT reach obs_m2 even though the callbacks are equal
obs_m2 survives a stale disposer belonging to an equal callable — this is lazily-py's b2de504 defect
cell teardown drops observers without invoking them — disposal is not a final notification
a disposer outliving its cell is a no-op; teardown paths do not control their own order
